title: Last Exit to Brooklyn
description: A grim novel about violence, poverty, sexuality, and social wreckage in postwar Brooklyn.

cover.alt: Last Exit to Brooklyn cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A grim novel about violence, poverty, sexuality, and social wreckage in postwar Brooklyn.
banningParagraphs: Last Exit to Brooklyn entered censorship debates as a novel associated with violence, sexuality, and working class life. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and sexual explicitness.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1960s in United Kingdom, where British courts convicted publisher for obscenity. The book was prosecuted and suppressed before later legal reversal. Its trajectory parallels other mid-century fights over literary realism and obscenity.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
